topic: Someone discovers their friend has been lying about their job
raw_output:
[story 1]

The lukewarm coffee tasted like ash in my mouth. I stared at the screen, the meticulously crafted LinkedIn profile of Liam mocking me with its polished veneer. Every carefully chosen word felt like a deliberate falsehood, a carefully constructed wall I was now realizing was paper-thin. I’d known Liam for years, shared late-night pizza runs and whispered anxieties, but this… this was a chasm. When I finally pressed him about the ‘senior analyst’ title, his smile had been too wide, his eyes flicking away just a fraction too quickly. My hands started shaking, the cheap plastic mug rattling against the desk. It wasn't just the lie; it was the realization that the foundation of our shared history had been built on shifting sand. I leaned back in my chair, the springs groaning under my weight, and just let the silence settle, heavy and suffocating.

[story 2]

I kept tracing the lines of the contract, the official documentation that suddenly felt like evidence in a poorly staged drama. My stomach twisted into a tight knot, a cold, heavy pressure that radiated outward. For weeks, I’d been trying to ignore the growing sense of unease, chalking it up to stress, to my own overactive imagination. But seeing the discrepancy between the story Liam told and the sparse, vague details he offered felt like a physical blow. When he finally admitted the truth, the air in the cramped apartment seemed to thin out. I didn't cry; instead, I just stood up, walked to the window, and watched the city lights blur into streaks of indifferent color. My limbs felt weighted, moving through the motions of everyday life with a sluggish, leaden quality.

[story 3]

The phone call ended, leaving a ringing silence that echoed in my ears. I sat on the edge of the bed, pulling my knees up to my chest, trying to make myself small. Every muscle in my body felt tense, coiled tight, as if bracing for an impact that never quite materialized. Liam’s casual dismissal of my concerns—that I was ‘being too sensitive’—was the final, sharp edge. I looked down at my hands, noticing the faint tremor that had started in my fingers and now seemed to vibrate through my entire frame. The energy that usually propelled me forward just evaporated, leaving behind a hollow, echoing emptiness. It was as if the color had been leached out of everything, leaving only a muted, grey existence.
